summary: Strong second‑set jams, mixed guitar mix, solid first set
blurb: The show delivers a powerful Shakedown Street sandwich and an intense Space → Other One → Wharf Rat sequence. Jerry’s playing is uneven—winded at times but soaring in the improvisational peaks. The audience tape captures the energy better than the problematic soundboard mixes.
review: Alpine Valley on June 29, 1986 offered a night of high‑octane Dead magic tempered by some technical hiccups. The first set opened with the rolling groove of Mississippi Half‑Step Uptown Toodeloo into Franklin’s Tower and a fiery Dancin’ In The Street before winding down with a classic Samson & Delilah closer. The second set was where the show truly ignited: a Shakedown Street sandwich launched into an electric Space → The Other One → Wharf Rat stretch that had the band “heroic” in their drum interplay and pushed Jerry’s guitar to soaring heights, even if his tone got buried on several tracks. Not Fade Away closed the night with a seamless transition that fans still cite as a highlight. While the soundboard recordings suffer from a thin, underwater drum mix and an especially low Jerry level on Shakedown Street, Estimated Prophet, and Eyes of the World, the audience‑sourced tape (gd86-06-29.aud.eD.12969.sbeok.shnf) captures the live energy and presents the most balanced listening experience.

rating_calculation.sentiment_rationale: The six recordings show mixed sentiment overall – a few positive notes about the jams and drummers, but also criticisms of Jerry's mix and occasional vocal fatigue. No consistent strong praise or criticism across multiple sources, so no adjustment is applied.

best_recording.reason: Fair‑quality audience tape that captures the concert’s energy and includes all standout moments, avoiding the low‑mix guitar issues of the soundboard sources.

key_highlights: Shakedown Street sandwich launches a high‑energy second set
Space → The Other One → Wharf Rat improvisation shines despite mix challenges
Half‑Step > Franklin's Tower > Dancin' In The Street sequence delivers tight jamming
Samson & Delilah provides a classic first‑set closer

band_performance.Jerry: Jerry’s playing was uneven—he sounded a bit winded and his guitar often got buried in the mix, yet he delivered strong moments, especially the soaring solo through Space → The Other One → Wharf Rat.
band_performance.Phil: Phil kept a solid bass foundation and helped drive the set's energy, pushing the band harder on the brisk second half.
band_performance.Bob: Bob’s vocals rose to an intense level during the Space‑Other One‑Wharf Rat stretch and he guided quick transitions into Wharf Rat.
band_performance.Mickey: Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann’s interplay was a highlight, delivering heroic communication throughout; however, the mix sometimes made drums sound thin or underwater.
band_performance.Billy: Mickey Hart and Bill Kreutzmann’s interplay was a highlight, delivering heroic communication throughout; however, the mix sometimes made drums sound thin or underwater.
